source_metadata.title_raw: Edward J. Kiernan - A-18-100 - September 17, 2018 - Yreka

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Edward J. Kiernan 
County Counsel, Siskiyou County 
P.O. Box 659 
205 Lane Street 
Yreka, California 96097 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-18-100 
Dear Mr. Kiernan: 
September 17, 2018 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding Government Code Section 1090, et 
seq. 1 We are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and 
any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the 
facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice. 
Regarding our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all 
pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Siskiyou County 
District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097. l(c)(3).) We did not receive a 
written response from either entity. (Section 1097.l(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, 
for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding 
against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.l(c)(5).) 
QUESTION 
Does Section 1090 prohibit the design consultant firm Nacht & Lewis from entering into a 
new agreement with Siskiyou County (the "County"), either by renewing an expired contract on the 
same project or, alternatively, by entering into a new contract if selected after a Request for 
Proposal ("RFP"), to prepare plans and specifications (the "Design Work") for a new AB900 
fonded jail project at the Juvenile Hall site and provide related architectural services through 
construction, given the scope of services Nacht & Lewis provided the County under the prior 
contracts? 
CONCLUSION 
No. Section 1090 does not prohibit the County from entering a contract with Nacht & Lewis 
on the Juvenile Hall site because, based on the facts provided, Nacht & Lewis was a subcontractor 
that provided technical analysis and not subject to Section 1090. Nacht & Lewis did not impose 
considerable influence over the County regarding the new contract, has derived no unfair advantage 
in procuring the new contract by the work it performed under the first contract, and did not 
participate in the making of a new contract through its performance of the first contract. 
1 All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS AS PROVIDED BY THE REQUESTOR 
A. 
Realignment Requirements. 
File No. A-18-100 
Page No. 2 
In 2011, new law was enacted under AB 109 and AB 117 that required counties to house 
newly-convicted low-level criminal offenders without current or prior serious or violent offenses in 
county jails (Realignment). Under Realignment, these low-level offenders are no longer housed in 
State prisons, and county jails are required to provide recidivism reduction programs, such as job 
training, reentry skills, drug and alcohol counseling, and the facilities to provide those services. 
In June 2007, in anticipation of Realignment law, the Siskiyou County Board of Supervisors 
authorized the Siskiyou County Jail to enter a contract with TRG Consulting to complete a Jail 
Needs Assessment for future funding to construct a new jail. 
B. 
Assessment, Funding and Contract with TRG Consulting. 
In October 2011, the County contracted with TRG Consulting to update their 2007 Jail 
Needs Assessment in preparation to apply for Phase II AB900 (the project) funding, and to prepare 
a letter of intent on behalf of the County to apply for project funding. The Jail Needs Assessment 
recommended building a 180-bedjail facility. 
On January 11, 2012, the County submitted its application to the State Department of 
Corrections for project funding, requesting $26,985,416. On November 8, 2012, the Board of State 
and Community Corrections ("BSCC") announced that the County had received a conditional 
partial award of project funds in the amount of $24,140,000. The County accepted the partial award. 
However, this conditional partial award required the County to amend its project application to a 
150-bed jail facility and request an extension of time to establish the project with the BSCC. 
On January 17, 2013, the BSCC approved the County's request for an extension of time and 
to amend the County's project application to reduce the size of the project to a 150-bedjail facility. 
From January 2013 to September 2013, the County acquired property where the new 150-bedjail 
facility was proposed to be built, referred to as the Foothill site: 
C. 
Contract with Nacht & Lewis for Design Work and Project Feasibility. 
In September 2013, the County issued an RFP soliciting proposals from architects to prepare 
Design Work for the project at the Foothill site. Included as the first task in the scope of work was 
preparation of a Feasibility Study to determine if the project could be built within the available 
budget. Nacht & Lewis submitted a proposal to the RFP and in April 2014, it was awarded the 
contract to perform the Design Work for the project at the Foothill site within the project budget of 
$24,140,000. 
In July 2015, the BSCC announced the County was awarded an additional conditional 
project fund in the amount of its original request of $26,985,416. This required the County to 
amend its project application to increase the size and scope of the County's proposed new jail 
project back to its original proposal of a 180-bed jail facility and request an extension of time to 


File No. A-18-100 
Page No. 3 
"establish" the project with the BSCC. Based on these changes, the County's contract with Nacht & 
Lewis was amended three times to provide for additional funds, services and to extend the 
contract's termination date to June 30, 2016. 
During the contract term, Nacht & Lewis completed only a feasibility study, the first task in 
the scope of work. Based on that study, the County determined that a new 180-bedjail at the 
Foothill site was not feasible within the budget. The County had no additional funds for the new jail 
project. Consequently, the County Board submitted a special sales tax ballot measure to pay for the 
new jail on June 7, 2016, which the voters did not approve. The County Board then submitted a 
general sales tax ballot measure on November 8, 2016, which the voters also did not approve. 
The contract between the County and Nacht & Lewis expired on June 30, 2016, due to a 
lack of sufficient funding to construct the project at the Foothill site. 
D. 
Contract with Vanir and the Juvenile Hall Site. 
In March 2017, the County considered converting and remodeling the existing 40-bed 
County Juvenile Hall facility (the new project). To determine if this was feasible, the County issued 
an RFP to solicit bids from a consultant to conduct a Feasibility Study on whether the existing 
Juvenile Hall facility could be converted and remodeled within the existing budget of $26,985,416, 
and to verify that the Foothill site could not be built within that budget. 
On April 21, 2017, Vanir submitted a proposal to the RFP for a Feasibility Study, with 
Nacht & Lewis as a sub-consultant (the subcontract). On May 10, 2017, the County awarded the 
contract to Vanir, with the following scope of work: 
1. Contractor shall provide an analysis of the juvenile detention conversion including 
confirmation of County juvenile detention needs and possible relocation options. 
2. Contractor shall provide a determination and confirmation ofBSCC requirements regarding 
upgrades to the juvenile hall for juveniles as part of partial conversion to adult, as well as 
code limitations of co-location of juvenile and adult. 
3. Contractor shall provide a determination of building code upgrades and state requirements if 
state funds are used for conversion of an existing facility including safety and security 
upgrades. 
4. Contractor shall provide an analysis of how a new housing unit could be added to the 
existing facility to accommodate the jail's population and the needed inmate and staff 
support services. 
5. Contractor shall provide an analysis of the site and utility upgrades that will be required to 
support an expanded secure facility. 
6. Contractor shall provide estimating support as part of the analysis of scope options to bring 
the project within budget. Estimates shall include the cost of upgrading the entire juvenile 
detention facility including a new addition. Estimating shall include the 3 page estimate 
necessary for BSCC approval of revised scope. 
Under the subcontract, as sub-consultants to Vanir, Nacht & Lewis assisted with tasks 3 and 
4 above, providing only technical analysis. Nacht & Lewis staff toured the existing juvenile hall, 


File No. A-18-100 
Page No. 4 
completed a building code analysis of the existing building, and identified potential fire and life 
safety issues, including accessibility and security upgrades that would be required. Nacht & Lewis 
also analyzed the County-owned property and determined that there was an adequate site area for a 
new housing unit addition. Nacht & Lewis staff was directed by Vanir's project manager and had 
limited communication with County staff. 
In September 2017, the BSCC further extended the project timeline to May 2018, to change 
the site location to the existing Juvenile Hall facility, and to change the project scope to remodel the 
existing facility and to add a housing unit to accommodate approximately 160 inmates, which 
would minimize the scale and cost of the original proposed project. The Vanir contract was 
amended accordingly to extend the expiration date of the contract to November 30, 2018, to expand 
the scope of work to include taking the Juvenile Hall new jail project if the project was determined 
to be feasible, to provide conceptual drawings of the Juvenile Hall new jail project, and to provide 
CEQA documentation for the project. Nacht & Lewis does not have a contractual agreement with 
Vanir to provide services under the expanded scope of these amendments. In February 2018, the 
County decided to enter into a contract with a different consultant for the CEQA work. 
On February 21, 2018, the County requested an additional extension of time from the BSCC 
to from May 2018, to December 2018, to allow time to complete the CEQA analysis and formalize 
the project with the BSCC. On April 19, 2018, the BSCC Board met regarding the County's request 
for an additional extension. 
The County will issue an RFP for design work and architectural services through 
construction at the Juvenile Hall site. 
ANALYSIS 
A. 
Independent Contractors under Section 1090. 
Interpreting the term "employees" as used in Section 1090, the California Supreme Court 
recently affirmed the long-standing rule from case law that independent contractors are not 
categorically excluded from Section 1090: "Liability under the statute can extend to independent 
contractors who have duties to engage in or advise on public contracting." (People v. Superior 
Court (Sahlolbei) (2017) 3 Cal.5th 230, 233.) For example, an independent contractor for a state or 
local government agency that "has a hand in designing and developing the plans and specifications 
for the project," has made or participated in the making of a contract for the construction of the 
project and is therefore prohibited from entering a contract to complete the project. (Davis v. Fresno 
Unified School District (2015) 237 Cal.App.4th 261, 300-301.) 
However, not all independent contractors are covered by Section 1090. (Sahlolbei, supra, at 
p. 240.) '"An individual's status as an official under [Section 1090] turns on the extent to which the 
person influences an agency's contracting decisions or otherwise acts in a capacity that demands the 
public trust."' (Ibid, quoting Hub City Solid Waste Services, Inc. v. City of Compton (Hub City) 
(2010) 186 Cal.App.4th 1114, 1125.) In Hub City, the court held that an independent contractor that 
exerts "considerable influence over the contracting decisions of a public agency" is subject to 
Section 1090. (Hub City, suprf!, at pp. 1124-1125.) 


File No. A-18-100 
Page No. 5 
Previously, we have advised that consultants who play a limited technical role and are 
removed from influencing the contracting decisions of a public agency may not be subject to 
Section 1090. (La Salle Advice Letter, No. A-17-074 and Green Advice Letter, No. A-16-084.) 
Likewise, in the Chadwick Advice Letter, No. A-15-147, we determined that only the primary 
consultant and a "highly involved sub-consultant" were subject to Section 1090. The sub-
consultants who provided technical input, reports, and similar information in a support role to the 
consultants, were not subject to Section 1090. 
Generally, an independent contractor is prohibited from a subsequent project to complete a 
project only if the contractor has had extensive involvement in the initial design and qevelop of the 
project and the nature of the contractor's involvement gives the contractor an unfair advantage in 
the subsequent award of the contract. 
B. 
Nacht & Lewis is Not Subject to Section 1090. 
The facts provided show that Nacht & Lewis was a primary consultant for project design 
and feasibility at the Foothill site. The County determined in 2016 that construction of the project at 
the Foothill site was not feasible based on the feasibility study by Nacht & Lewis. Thus, based on 
its own study, Nacht & Lewis derived no unfair advantage in the subsequent award of a contract on 
the project at the Foothill site. To the contrary, the Nacht & Lewis study helped to eliminate any 
subsequent contracts at this site. Under the circumstances, work performed under the Foothill site 
contract provided Nacht & Lewis with no advantage whatsoever in obtaining the subsequent award 
of a contract on the project. 
In March 2017, the County, not Nacht & Lewis, identified the Juvenile Hall site as a 
potentially feasible option for the project. The County awarded the feasibility study contract to 
Vanir, not Nacht & Lewis. As a Vanir subcontractor for the study, Nacht & Lewis provided only 
technical analysis and its staff was directed by Vanir's project manager. These facts demonstrate 
that on the Juvenile Hall site study, Nacht & Lewis: (1) was not a "highly involved sub-consultant;" 
(2) provided technical input and reports in a support role to Vanir; and (3) did not exert influence 
over the County's contracting decisions. 
The facts show that Nacht & Lewis performed work of a limited, technical nature as a sub-
consultant with Vanir at the Juvenile Hall site. There are no facts to support that Nacht & Lewis 
was engaged in the development of the RFP, or had the potential to exert considerable influence in 
the County's contracting decisions. Moreover, Nacht & Lewis derived no apparent advantage 
regarding the Juvenile Hall site for the work performed on the Foothill site. Nacht & Lewis had no 
unfair advantage in obtaining a subsequent contract for work on the Juvenile Hall site because of 
the technical subcontract work it performed for Vanir on the feasibility study. The facts do not show 
Nacht & Lewis engaged in any planning, preliminary discussions or negotiations with the County 
regarding the Juvenile Hall site. Nacht & Lewis has no advantage over other potential respondents 
in procuring a contract to perform plans, specifications, and related architectural services through 
construction for the project at the Juvenile Hall site. 


File No. A-18-100 
Page No. 6 
If you have other questions on this matter, then please contact me at (916) 327-5525. 
JMFJr.:jgl 
Sincerely, 
BrianG. Lau 
Acting General Counsel 
r Counsel, Legal Division

sections.question:
Does Section 1090 prohibit the design consultant firm Nacht & Lewis from entering into a 
new agreement with Siskiyou County (the "County"), either by renewing an expired contract on the 
same project or, alternatively, by entering into a new contract if selected after a Request for

construction, given the scope of services Nacht & Lewis provided the County under the prior 
contracts?
sections.conclusion:
No. Section 1090 does not prohibit the County from entering a contract with Nacht & Lewis 
on the Juvenile Hall site because, based on the facts provided, Nacht & Lewis was a subcontractor 
that provided technical analysis and not subject to Section 1090. Nacht & Lewis did not impose 
considerable influence over the County regarding the new contract, has derived no unfair advantage 
in procuring the new contract by the work it performed under the first contract, and did not 
participate in the making of a new contract through its performance of the first contract. 
1 FACTS AS PROVIDED BY THE REQUESTOR 
A. 
Realignment Requirements.  
In 2011, new law was enacted under AB 109 and AB 117 that required counties to house 
newly-convicted low-level criminal offenders without current or prior serious or violent offenses in

B. 
Assessment, Funding and Contract with TRG Consulting. 
In October 2011, the County contracted with TRG Consulting to update their 2007 Jail 
Needs Assessment in preparation to apply for Phase II AB900 (the project) funding, and to prepare 
a letter of intent on behalf of the County to apply for project funding. The Jail Needs Assessment 
recommended building a 180-bedjail facility. 
On January 11, 2012, the County submitted its application to the State Department of

In July 2015, the BSCC announced the County was awarded an additional conditional

sections.analysis:
A. 
Independent Contractors under Section 1090. 
Interpreting the term "employees" as used in Section 1090, the California Supreme Court 
recently affirmed the long-standing rule from case law that independent contractors are not 
categorically excluded from Section 1090: "Liability under the statute can extend to independent 
contractors who have duties to engage in or advise on public contracting." (People v. Superior 
Court (Sahlolbei) (2017) 3 Cal.5th 230, 233.) For example, an independent contractor for a state or 
local government agency that "has a hand in designing and developing the plans and specifications 
for the project," has made or participated in the making of a contract for the construction of the 
project and is therefore prohibited from entering a contract to complete the project. (Davis v. Fresno 
Unified School District (2015) 237 Cal.App.4th 261, 300-301.) 
However, not all independent contractors are covered by Section 1090. (Sahlolbei, supra, at 
p. 240.) '"An individual's status as an official under [Section 1090] turns on the extent to which the 
person influences an agency's contracting decisions or otherwise acts in a capacity that demands the 
public trust."' (Ibid, quoting Hub City Solid Waste Services, Inc. v. City of Compton (Hub City) 
(2010) 186 Cal.App.4th 1114, 1125.) In Hub City, the court held that an independent contractor that 
exerts "considerable influence over the contracting decisions of a public agency" is subject to

Previously, we have advised that consultants who play a limited technical role and are

Lewis was amended three times to provide for additional funds, services and to extend the 
contract's termination date to June 30, 2016. 
During the contract term, Nacht & Lewis completed only a feasibility study, the first task in 
the scope of work. Based on that study, the County determined that a new 180-bedjail at the 
Foothill site was not feasible within the budget. The County had no additional funds for the new jail 
project. Consequently, the County Board submitted a special sales tax ballot measure to pay for the 
new jail on June 7, 2016, which the voters did not approve. The County Board then submitted a 
general sales tax ballot measure on November 8, 2016, which the voters also did not approve. 
The contract between the County and Nacht & Lewis expired on June 30, 2016, due to a 
lack of sufficient funding to construct the project at the Foothill site. 
D. 
Contract with Vanir and the Juvenile Hall Site. 
In March 2017, the County considered converting and remodeling the existing 40-bed 
County Juvenile Hall facility (the new project). To determine if this was feasible, the County issued 
an RFP to solicit bids from a consultant to conduct a Feasibility Study on whether the existing 
Juvenile Hall facility could be converted and remodeled within the existing budget of $26,985,416, 
and to verify that the Foothill site could not be built within that budget. 
On April 21, 2017, Vanir submitted a proposal to the RFP for a Feasibility Study, with 
Nacht & Lewis as a sub-consultant (the subcontract). On May 10, 2017, the County awarded the 
contract to Vanir, with the following scope of work: 
1. Contractor shall provide an analysis of the juvenile detention conversion including 
confirmation of County juvenile detention needs and possible relocation options. 
2. Contractor shall provide a determination and confirmation ofBSCC requirements regarding 
upgrades to the juvenile hall for juveniles as part of partial conversion to adult, as well as 
code limitations of co-location of juvenile and adult. 
3. Contractor shall provide a determination of building code upgrades and state requirements if 
state funds are used for conversion of an existing facility including safety and security 
upgrades. 
4. Contractor shall provide an analysis of how a new housing unit could be added to the 
existing facility to accommodate the jail's population and the needed inmate and staff 
support services. 
5. Contractor shall provide an analysis of the site and utility upgrades that will be required to 
support an expanded secure facility. 
6. Contractor shall provide estimating support as part of the analysis of scope options to bring 
the project within budget. Estimates shall include the cost of upgrading the entire juvenile 
detention facility including a new addition. Estimating shall include the 3 page estimate 
necessary for BSCC approval of revised scope. 
Under the subcontract, as sub-consultants to Vanir, Nacht & Lewis assisted with tasks 3 and 
4 above, providing only technical analysis. Nacht & Lewis staff toured the existing juvenile hall,  
completed a building code analysis of the existing building, and identified potential fire and life 
safety issues, including accessibility and security upgrades that would be required. Nacht & Lewis 
also analyzed the County-owned property and determined that there was an adequate site area for a 
new housing unit addition. Nacht & Lewis staff was directed by Vanir's project manager and had 
limited communication with County staff. 
In September 2017, the BSCC further extended the project timeline to May 2018, to change 
the site location to the existing Juvenile Hall facility, and to change the project scope to remodel the 
existing facility and to add a housing unit to accommodate approximately 160 inmates, which 
would minimize the scale and cost of the original proposed project. The Vanir contract was 
amended accordingly to extend the expiration date of the contract to November 30, 2018, to expand 
the scope of work to include taking the Juvenile Hall new jail project if the project was determined 
to be feasible, to provide conceptual drawings of the Juvenile Hall new jail project, and to provide 
CEQA documentation for the project. Nacht & Lewis does not have a contractual agreement with 
Vanir to provide services under the expanded scope of these amendments. In February 2018, the 
County decided to enter into a contract with a different consultant for the CEQA work. 
On February 21, 2018, the County requested an additional extension of time from the BSCC 
to from May 2018, to December 2018, to allow time to complete the CEQA analysis and formalize 
the project with the BSCC. On April 19, 2018, the BSCC Board met regarding the County's request 
for an additional extension. 
The County will issue an RFP for design work and architectural services through 
construction at the Juvenile Hall site.
embedding.first_500_words: STATE OF CALIFORNIA FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 Edward J. Kiernan County Counsel, Siskiyou County P.O. Box 659 205 Lane Street Yreka, California 96097 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-18-100 Dear Mr. Kiernan: September 17, 2018 This letter responds to your request for advice regarding Government Code Section 1090, et seq. 1 We are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice. Regarding our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Siskiyou County District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097. l(c)(3).) We did not receive a written response from either entity. (Section 1097.l(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.l(c)(5).) QUESTION Does Section 1090 prohibit the design consultant firm Nacht & Lewis from entering into a new agreement with Siskiyou County (the "County"), either by renewing an expired contract on the same project or, alternatively, by entering into a new contract if selected after a Request for Proposal ("RFP"), to prepare plans and specifications (the "Design Work") for a new AB900 fonded jail project at the Juvenile Hall site and provide related architectural services through construction, given the scope of services Nacht & Lewis provided the County under the prior contracts? CONCLUSION No. Section 1090 does not prohibit the County from entering a contract with Nacht & Lewis on the Juvenile Hall site because, based on the facts provided, Nacht & Lewis was a subcontractor that provided technical analysis and not subject to Section 1090. Nacht & Lewis did not impose considerable influence over the County regarding the new contract, has derived no unfair advantage in procuring the new contract by the work it performed under the first contract, and did not participate in the making of a new contract through its performance of the first contract. 1 All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. FACTS AS PROVIDED BY THE REQUESTOR A. Realignment Requirements. File No. A-18-100 Page No. 2 In 2011, new law was enacted under AB 109 and AB 117 that required counties to house newly-convicted low-level criminal offenders without current or prior serious or violent offenses in county jails (Realignment). Under Realignment, these low-level offenders are no longer housed in State prisons, and county jails are required to provide recidivism reduction programs, such as job training, reentry skills, drug and alcohol counseling, and the facilities to provide those services. In June 2007, in anticipation of Realignment
